snp3.12:1.2: on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī in the Eastern Monastery, the palace of Migāra’s mother.
snp3.12:1.3:
Now on that occasion—the uposatha day of the fifteenth, a perfect full-moon night—
the Blessed One was sitting in the open air surrounded by the Saṅgha of monks.
snp3.12:1.4: Surveying the silent Saṅgha of monks, he addressed them:
snp3.12:2.1:
“Monks, if there are any who ask,
‘Your listening to teachings that are skillful, noble, leading onward,
going to self-awakening is a prerequisite for what?’ they should be told,
snp3.12:2.2: ‘For the sake of knowing qualities of dualities as they actually are.’
snp3.12:2.3: Which duality are you speaking about?
snp3.12:3.1:
‘This is stress.
This is the origination of stress’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:3.2:
‘This is the cessation of stress.
This is the path of practice leading to the cessation of stress’:
This is a second contemplation.
snp3.12:3.3: For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
snp3.12:3.4: either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
snp3.12:4.1: That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:4.2: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:
snp3.12:5.6: the way to the stilling of stress:
snp3.12:7.6: the way to the stilling of stress:
snp3.12:8.4: they aren’t headed to birth & aging.
snp3.12:9.1:
“Now, if there are any who ask,
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
snp3.12:9.4:
‘Whatever stress comes into play is all from acquisition as a requisite condition’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:9.5:
‘From the remainderless fading & cessation of that very acquisition, there is no coming into play of stress’:
This is a second contemplation.
snp3.12:9.6:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:9.7: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:
snp3.12:10.1: “From acquisition as cause the many forms of stress come into being in the world.
snp3.12:10.4: —the dullard— comes to stress again & again.
snp3.12:10.5: Therefore, discerning, you shouldn’t create acquisitions
snp3.12:10.6: as you stay focused on the birth & origin of stress.”
snp3.12:11.1:
“Now, if there are any who ask,
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
snp3.12:11.4:
‘Whatever stress comes into play is all from ignorance as a requisite condition’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:11.5:
‘From the remainderless fading & cessation of that very ignorance, there is no coming into play of stress’:
This is a second contemplation.
snp3.12:11.6:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:11.7: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:
snp3.12:12.1: “Those who journey the wandering-on through birth & death, again & again,
snp3.12:12.3: in this state here or anywhere else,
snp3.12:12.4: that destination is simply through ignorance.
snp3.12:13.1: This ignorance is a great delusion
snp3.12:13.2: whereby they have wandered-on a long, long time.
snp3.12:13.3: While beings immersed in clear knowing
snp3.12:14.1:
“Now, if there are any who ask,
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
‘There would.’
snp3.12:14.3:
‘Whatever stress comes into play is all from fabrication as a requisite condition’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:14.4:
‘From the remainderless fading & cessation of that very fabrication, there is no coming into play of stress’:
This is a second contemplation.
snp3.12:14.5:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:14.6: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:

snp3.12:16.6: Knowing this as it actually is,
snp3.12:17.4:
go to no further becoming.
“Now, if there are any who ask,

For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.

snp3.12:20.4:
free from hunger is totally unbound.
“Now, if there are any who ask,
snp3.12:21.1:
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
‘There would.’
snp3.12:21.3:
‘Whatever stress comes into play is all from contact as a requisite condition’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:21.4:
‘From the remainderless fading & cessation of that very contact, there is no coming into play of stress’:

snp3.12:21.5:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:

That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:21.6: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:
snp3.12:22.2: flowing along in the stream of becoming,
snp3.12:22.4: the ending of fetters is far away.
snp3.12:23.4:
free from hunger, are totally unbound.
“Now, if there are any who ask,
snp3.12:24.1:
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
‘There would.’
snp3.12:24.3:
‘Whatever stress comes into play is all from feeling as a requisite condition’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:24.4:
‘From the remainderless fading & cessation of that very feeling, there is no coming into play of stress’:
This is a second contemplation.
snp3.12:24.5:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:24.6: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:
snp3.12:26.3: seeing its deceptive nature, its dissolving, its passing away at each contact, each contact,
snp3.12:26.5: With just the ending of feeling,
snp3.12:26.6:
there is no stress coming into play.
“Now, if there are any who ask,
snp3.12:27.1:
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
‘There would.’
snp3.12:27.3:
‘Whatever stress comes into play is all from craving as a requisite condition’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:27.4:
‘From the remainderless fading & cessation of that very craving, there is no coming into play of stress’:
This is a second contemplation.
snp3.12:27.5:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:27.6: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:
snp3.12:28.2: wanders on a long, long time.
snp3.12:28.3: Neither in this state here nor anywhere else
snp3.12:28.4: does he go beyond the wandering- on.
snp3.12:29.2: that craving brings stress into play—
snp3.12:29.3: free from craving, devoid of clinging,
snp3.12:29.4:
mindful, the monk lives the mendicant life.”
“Now, if there are any who ask,
snp3.12:30.1:
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
‘There would.’
snp3.12:30.3:
‘Whatever stress comes into play is all from clinging as a requisite condition’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:30.4:
‘From the remainderless fading & cessation of that very clinging, there is no coming into play of stress’:
This is a second contemplation.
snp3.12:30.5:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:30.6: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:
snp3.12:31.1: “From clinging as a requisite condition comes becoming.
snp3.12:31.2: One who has come into being goes to stress.
snp3.12:31.3: There is death for one who is born.
snp3.12:31.4: This is the coming into play of stress.
snp3.12:32.1: Thus, with the ending of clinging, the wise
snp3.12:32.3: directly knowing the ending of birth,
snp3.12:32.4:
go to no further becoming.
“Now, if there are any who ask,
snp3.12:33.1:
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
‘There would.’
snp3.12:33.3:
‘Whatever stress comes into play is all from arousal as a requisite condition’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:33.4:
‘From the remainderless fading & cessation of that very arousal, there is no coming into play of stress’:
This is a second contemplation.
snp3.12:33.5:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:33.6: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:

snp3.12:34.4: there is no stress coming into play.

snp3.12:35.3: with the relinquishing of all arousal,
snp3.12:36.3: his wandering-on in birth totally ended:
snp3.12:37.1:
“Now, if there are any who ask,
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
‘There would.’
snp3.12:37.3:
‘Whatever stress comes into play is all from nutriment as a requisite condition’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:37.4:
‘From the remainderless fading & cessation of that very nutriment, there is no coming into play of stress’:
This is a second contemplation.
snp3.12:37.5:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:37.6: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:

snp3.12:41.1:
“Now, if there are any who ask,
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
‘There would.’
snp3.12:41.3:
‘Whatever stress comes into play is all from what is perturbed as a requisite condition’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:41.4:
‘From the remainderless fading & cessation of what is perturbed, there is no coming into play of stress’:
This is a second contemplation.
snp3.12:41.5:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:41.6: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:

snp3.12:44.1:
“Now, if there are any who ask,
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
‘There would.’
snp3.12:44.3:
‘For one who is dependent, there is wavering’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:44.4:
‘One who is independent doesn’t waver’:
This is a second contemplation.
snp3.12:44.5:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:44.6: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:

snp3.12:47.1:
“Now, if there are any who ask,
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
‘There would.’
snp3.12:47.3:
‘Formless phenomena are more peaceful than forms’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:47.4:

snp3.12:47.5:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.

snp3.12:50.1:
“Now, if there are any who ask,
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
‘There would.’
snp3.12:50.3:
‘Whatever is considered as “This is true” by the world with its devas,
Māras, & Brahmās, by this generation with its contemplatives & brahmans,
its royalty & commonfolk,
is rightly seen as it has come to be with right discernment by the noble ones as “This is false”’:
This is one contemplation.
snp3.12:50.4:
‘Whatever is considered as “This is false” by the world with its devas,
Māras, & Brahmās, by this generation with its contemplatives & brahmans,
its royalty & commonfolk,
is rightly seen as it has come to be with right discernment by the noble ones as “This is true”’:
This is a second contemplation.
snp3.12:50.5:
For a monk rightly contemplating this duality in this way—heedful, ardent, & resolute—one of two fruits can be expected:
either gnosis right here-&-now, or—if there be any remnant of clinging-sustenance—non-return.”
That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:50.6: Having said that, the One Well-Gone, the Teacher, said further:
snp3.12:51.1: “See the world, together with its devas,
snp3.12:52.1: In whatever terms they suppose it
snp3.12:52.2: it turns into something other than that,
snp3.12:52.3: and that’s what’s false about it:
snp3.12:53.2: That the noble ones know as true.
snp3.12:53.3: They, through breaking through to the truth,
snp3.12:54.1:
“Now, if there are any who ask,
‘Would there be the right contemplation of dualities in yet another way?’ they should be told,
snp3.12:54.4:

its royalty & commonfolk,

snp3.12:59.1: See the Dhamma, hard to understand!
snp3.12:59.2: Here those who don’t know are confused.
snp3.12:59.3: For those who are veiled, it’s darkness,
snp3.12:59.4: blindness for those who don’t see.
snp3.12:60.1: But for the good it is blatant,
snp3.12:60.2: like light for those who see.
snp3.12:60.3: Though in its very presence, they don’t understand it—
snp3.12:60.4:
dumb animals, unadept in the Dhamma.
It’s not easy
snp3.12:61.1: for those overcome by passion for becoming,
snp3.12:61.2: flowing along in the stream of becoming,
snp3.12:61.4: to wake up to this Dhamma.

snp3.12:63.1: That is what the Blessed One said.
snp3.12:63.2: Gratified, the monks delighted in the Blessed One’s words.
snp3.12:63.3:
And while this explanation was being given, the minds of 60 monks,
through lack of clinging, were released from effluents.
